---
title: "Linking lab data by design"
subtitle: "A sampling-provenance proposal for future incidents"
format:
  html: default
  docx: default
format-links: false
---

The companion to **[★ Ingesting lab data](lab-ingest.qmd)** is this: *why* linking lab data to the
right event/case is hard today, and a concrete idea for making it easy going forward. The
how-to page covers what you do now; this page is about where we're headed.

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
[<i class="bi bi-file-earmark-word"></i> Download as Word (.docx)](sampling-provenance.docx){.btn .btn-primary .btn-sm role="button" download=""}
:::

::: {.callout-note title="This is a proposal, not a current feature"}
The **Sampling Event Code** described here isn't built yet — it's a design direction for
discussion. Everything you actually test today is on the [Ingesting lab data](lab-ingest.qmd)
page. Feedback on this idea is very welcome — see [Giving feedback](feedback.qmd).
:::

## The problem: a moving timeline

HAB response isn't a tidy pipeline. A typical incident plays out like this:

- An incident is **opened on partial information** — a report, a photo, a call. Whether anyone
  samples, and who, is often undecided.
- One or more parties — Water Board staff, a tribe, a lake association, a partner agency —
  **decide independently to sample**, each on their own schedule.
- Samples are collected, split, and shipped to **different laboratories**, each with its own
  forms, sample IDs, and turnaround.
- Results **trickle back about a week later**, under the lab's naming — not the program's.
- For a high-concern case, **more sampling events follow**, multiplying the artifacts.

Everything gets tucked into folders. Months or years later someone opens those folders and tries
to reconstruct which result belongs to which sampling event, which event to which case, and which
case to which response. That reconstruction is slow, ambiguous, and lossy — and it delays the
moment the data can become authoritative public information.

## Why it's hard now: we assign identity too late

The root cause, in database terms: **the key that ties a result to its incident doesn't exist at
the time the sample is taken.** It has to be rebuilt afterward from weak signals.

- **Fuzzy matching after the fact.** Identity is inferred from station code, date, coordinates,
  and water-body name. That's exactly what the **Lab data workboard** reconciler does today
  ("within ~2 km and ±14 days") — but it's a *backstop*, not ground truth. Two blooms on the same
  lake in the same fortnight are genuinely hard to tell apart from coordinates alone.
- **The CoC carries the only authoritative location and date** — and it arrives as paper or a
  rotated PDF someone has to read and retype, often long after the fact.
- **Every party uses its own IDs.** Field crew, each lab, and the case file name the same sample
  three different ways, with no key in common.
- **One sampling event is fragmented** — grab samples to Lab A, SPATT to Lab B, genetic assays to
  Lab C — with nothing declaring they belong together.

No amount of downstream cleverness fully recovers information that was never captured. The fix is
to move identity *upstream*, to the one moment every party passes through: the act of sampling.

## The proposal: one code, issued at sampling time

Issue a **Sampling Event Code (SEC)** the moment a sampling effort is first anticipated —
*before* you know the party, the sample count, or the analyses. It's a short, human-readable,
collision-free token that goes on **everything**: the sampling order, the CoC, the sample labels,
the lab submission, the returned results file, the folder and file names, and the case record.

``` text
FHAB-R5-20260731-7QK2
 │    │      │      └ random + check character (catches a mistype at entry)
 │    │      └ date the sampling event was opened
 │    └ regional board
 └ program
```

The format is **self-describing** so a person can sanity-check it at a glance, and **scope-free at
issue**: the responsible party, the number of samples, the analytes, and the destination labs all
attach to the code *as they materialize*. You never have to know the shape of the sampling effort
in advance — you only have to name it.

::: {.callout-tip title="It fits the model we already have"}
The SEC is just the human-facing face of the **Sampling Event** the system already creates for
each ingest (its `Sampling_Event_ID`, drawn from a reserved id range that can't collide with the
ids published on data.ca.gov). The SEC simply **pre-registers** that sampling event and pushes its
id upstream, so the batch exists *before* its data arrives instead of being created at ingest.
:::

One high-concern case naturally holds **many** sampling events over time — each new field effort
mints a new SEC under the same case:

``` text
Case
└─ Report / Event
   ├─ Sampling Event · SEC #1  →  Sample  →  Result
   └─ Sampling Event · SEC #2  →  Sample  →  Result
```

## The workflow: pre-register, then reconcile on arrival

The SEC flips today's order of operations. Instead of "collect data, then reconstruct identity,"
it becomes "declare identity, then collect data against it."

| When | What happens |
|---|---|
| **T0 — sampling anticipated** | A staffer clicks *Start a sampling event*. The system mints the SEC and produces a pre-stamped field packet — CoC header, labels, QR — carrying the SEC, region, and water body if known. Party and analyses left blank. |
| **Field** | Samples are labeled with the SEC; the CoC is pre-filled with it. Splits to different labs carry the same SEC plus a short sample suffix, so the fragments stay a family. |
| **Laboratory** | The SEC rides the submission and is **required back** on the deliverable — a field, or a filename convention. A one-line ask in a lab contract or SOP. |
| **≈ 1 week later** | Ingest matches on the SEC and auto-links every sample and result to the pre-registered event — and thus the case. Anything without a code falls to the workboard reconciler as the **exception**, not the default. |

> Move the identifier to the front of the timeline, and the reconciliation work that consumes
> staff months mostly disappears — because it never has to happen.

## Most of the machinery already exists

This is an increment on what's built, not a rebuild:

| Capability | Status | Role in the SEC workflow |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Event batch + `Sampling_Event_ID` | **In place** | Becomes the record the SEC pre-registers |
| Reserved id range (≥ 1e9) | **In place** | Mints SECs that never collide with published ids |
| Station registry (shared codes) | **In place** | Common location vocabulary for field & labs |
| Workboard reconciler (2 km / ±14 d) | **In place** | Fallback for uncoded / legacy data |
| Duplicate detect & merge | **In place** | Trivial once an SEC is present |
| Provenance & row-level audit | **In place** | Every SEC-linked ingest is attributable |
| Pre-issued SEC + printable field packet | *To add* | The front door of the workflow |
| Lab deliverable template + "return the code" clause | *To add* | Guarantees the code comes home |
| SEC auto-match on ingest | *To add* | Turns linking into a lookup |
| QR labels / mobile CoC capture | *Later* | Removes hand-transcription entirely |

## Habits that make the code stick

- **One code, every communication** — CoC, sample order, lab deliverable, folder and file names,
  plus a scannable QR for one-tap linking.
- **Standardize the deliverable** — ask labs for a CEDEN-aligned electronic file carrying the SEC
  and station code, instead of a PDF to transcribe. Provide a template; make it the default in
  agreements.
- **Capture the CoC as data at T0** — a mobile CoC form stamped with the SEC makes the
  authoritative location and date digital from minute one, ending the rotated-PDF-and-retype
  problem.
- **Share the station registry** — when field crews and labs draw codes from the same list,
  locations line up by construction.
- **Keep the reconciler forever** — legacy folders and the occasional un-coded submission always
  exist; the heuristic matcher stays the safety net beneath the code.

## Getting there — a phased rollout

1. **Mint & match (now).** Generate the SEC in the app, print it on a CoC cover sheet and sample
   labels, ask labs to return it, and auto-link on a match at ingest. Uses the sampling-event
   batch and reconciler that already exist — no field hardware.
2. **Scan & standardize.** QR labels and a mobile CoC form for digital capture at T0, plus a
   CEDEN-aligned deliverable template so results arrive machine-ready.
3. **Direct submission.** A portal or API for partners and labs to submit deliverables keyed by
   SEC — field to public data with no manual hop.

## Honest limits

- **Adoption depends on people using the code.** Make it the path of least resistance —
  pre-printed packets, a one-click issue button, a "return the code" clause in lab agreements —
  with the reconciler catching whatever slips through.
- **Parties who don't coordinate.** Whoever opens the incident issues and shares the SEC; where
  several parties sample independently, each event gets its own SEC and all reconcile cleanly
  under one case.
- **Legacy data.** Everything already in folders keeps flowing through the existing matcher; the
  SEC changes the future, not the past.

::: {.callout-important title="Recommendation"}
Adopt the **Sampling Event Code** as the linking spine: pre-register every sampling event and push
its code upstream to the field and the labs; standardize the electronic deliverable so the code
comes home; keep heuristic reconciliation as the exception path. The database is already built for
it — the SEC just captures, at the one moment every party shares, the small piece of information
we currently spend months trying to recover.
:::

## Further reading

Resources for the team to dive deeper. Each note says *why it's relevant to linking lab data*.

### California program & data standards

- [California Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) Portal](https://mywaterquality.ca.gov/habs/) — the
  statewide program hub this system supports.
- [CCHAB Network & Voluntary Guidance](https://mywaterquality.ca.gov/cyanohab/) — the recreational
  HAB response framework and the **cyanotoxin trigger levels** used to colour the Lab data map
  (Microcystins 0.8 / 6 / 20 µg/L, etc.).
- [SWAMP Freshwater CyanoHABs Program](https://www.waterboards.ca.gov/water_issues/programs/swamp/freshwater_cyanobacteria.html)
  — the Water Boards monitoring program context.
- [CEDEN — California Environmental Data Exchange Network](https://ceden.waterboards.ca.gov/) — the
  state exchange our lab chemistry is shaped to; the natural home for a standardized deliverable.
- [CEDEN data templates & business rules](https://ceden.waterboards.ca.gov/data-templates.html) —
  the electronic data deliverable (EDD) a *"return the SEC"* field would extend. Its **Chemistry
  template** is exactly the format the ingest workflow expects.

### National water-data exchange — align the SEC with these

- [EPA Water Quality Exchange (WQX) / Water Quality Data](https://www.epa.gov/waterdata/water-quality-data)
  — the national submission standard. Note its **Activity ID**, **Project ID**, and **Monitoring
  Location ID**: the SEC essentially plays the role of a program-issued *Project/Activity*
  identifier, so aligning with WQX keeps us interoperable.
- [WQX Web template files](https://www.epa.gov/waterdata/water-quality-exchange-web-template-files)
  — concrete templates that show how those identifiers travel with results.
- [Water Quality Portal (USGS + EPA)](https://www.waterqualitydata.us/) — the national discovery
  portal our published open data ultimately feeds.

### Persistent identifiers — the precedent for issuing an ID at sampling time

- [IGSN / SESAR — persistent identifiers for physical samples](https://www.geosamples.org/) — the
  closest real-world analog to the Sampling Event Code: a **globally unique ID registered when a
  physical sample is collected** (now issued via [DataCite](https://datacite.org/)). The same idea,
  applied to a bloom incident's sampling effort.
- [GeoConnex (Internet of Water)](https://geoconnex.us/) ([docs](https://docs.geoconnex.us/)) —
  persistent identifiers for hydrologic features (rivers, watersheds, monitoring sites); already
  used in FHAB for station and watershed URIs.
- [USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset (HUC-12)](https://www.usgs.gov/national-hydrography/watershed-boundary-dataset)
  — the authoritative watershed layer behind the crosswalk's HUC12 assignment.

### Data-management principle

- [FAIR data principles](https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/) — Findable, Accessible,
  Interoperable, Reusable. The SEC is, in effect, the tactic that makes a result **Findable** and
  **Interoperable** by construction instead of by reconciliation.
